key_insight: Ultrasound-driven microbubbles can penetrate dense fibrin networks and, through repeated sub-fracture oscillatory loading, progressively soften and damage the matrix rather than breaking fibers in a single event.
engineering_relevance: This provides a mechanistic basis for using acoustic cavitation to improve drug transport and thrombolysis in stiff, fibrotic blood clots.
method: Observed bubble–fibrin interactions under ultrasound-driven cavitation
Measured network mechanical response over thousands of loading cycles
Compared response across peak force levels to distinguish shakedown from progressive damage
evidence_signal: The abstract reports penetrated bubbles forming 3D transport paths, nanobead infiltration to about 200 �bm depth, and drastic dissipation losses with network softening over thousands of cycles.
watch_item: The reported benefits depend on repeated cyclic loading and force level, so translating this to therapy will require careful control to avoid unintended tissue damage or insufficient cavitation.
paper_title: Interaction mechanics of acoustic cavitation with fibrin networks
